title: Greystone Commercial Properties — HVAC Repair Capitalization

dataset.account: Work Order / Invoice — HVAC Compressor Replacement
dataset.notes: Like-for-like compressor unit; failed unit replaced with equivalent model

dataset.account: Correct Entry: Debit Repairs & Maintenance Expense (OpEx)
dataset.notes: Full $85K expensed in period; no depreciation tail created

dataset.account: Impact on Current-Period Operating Income (AI vs. Correct)
dataset.notes: AI approach overstates operating income by $85K this period; understates expense

aiNarrative: Greystone Commercial Properties submitted a $85,000 invoice for the replacement of an HVAC compressor unit at its Westfield Office Center property. Reviewing the work order, I note that the expenditure is significant — well above the company's $5,000 capitalization threshold — and involves a major mechanical component that is integral to the building's operating systems. Based on the dollar magnitude and the fact that the HVAC system is a structural component of the building asset, this expenditure extends the useful life of the building and qualifies as a capital improvement under ASC 360-10. I recommend recording a debit to the Building asset account for $85,000 and depreciating the component over 15 years (straight-line, ~$5,667 per year), consistent with MACRS guidance for HVAC systems. This treatment capitalizes the cost, preserves current-period operating income, and appropriately matches the expense to the future periods benefited by the improved asset. Recommend approving the capitalization.

hiddenTruth.correctIssueSummary: The AI incorrectly capitalized a like-for-like component replacement that merely restores original operating condition, violating ASC 360-10-25's betterment standard and overstating assets by $85,000 while understating current-period maintenance expense by the same amount.
hiddenTruth.expertFeedback: Under ASC 360-10-25-2, a cost is capitalized only if it extends the asset's useful life beyond original estimates, adds new capabilities, or represents a betterment — replacing a failed compressor with an equivalent unit does none of these. The work order explicitly describes a like-for-like replacement, which is restoration of original operating condition and must be expensed as maintenance. Capitalizing it inflates the building asset by $85,000, creates a fictitious 15-year depreciation stream, and flatters current-period operating income by the full $85,000. An experienced reviewer would pull the original equipment specifications alongside the replacement invoice to confirm the units are equivalent, then require a correcting entry debiting Repairs & Maintenance Expense and crediting the Building asset account.
evidenceToRequest: Original HVAC equipment specification sheet and installation records to confirm the replaced unit's model, capacity, and rated useful life
Vendor invoice and work order confirming the replacement compressor is a like-for-like equivalent (model number, BTU capacity, energy rating)
Company capitalization policy defining the criteria for betterment vs. maintenance
Prior-year GL entries for HVAC repairs to establish historical treatment of similar expenditures
